Vitamin B12 is the only vitamin that contains a metal ion: cobalt. This is why its chemical name is cobalamin.
The human body contains enough enzymes to catalyze over 37 million billion chemical reactions per second.
The brain uses about 20% of the body's total ATP (adenosine triphosphate) energy, despite being only 2% of the body's weight.
Scurvy is caused by Vitamin C deficiency, which stops the synthesis of collagen because the enzyme prolyl hydroxylase requires ascorbic acid as a cofactor.
Hemoglobin's affinity for oxygen drops significantly when blood pH decreases (like during heavy exercise). This is known as the Bohr effect.
Linoleic and alpha-linolenic acids are 'essential' fatty acids because the human body lacks the desaturase enzymes required to introduce double bonds beyond carbon 9.
The cycle of extracting energy from glucose is called the Krebs Cycle or Citric Acid Cycle, occurring in the mitochondrial matrix.
DNA polymerase makes a mistake approximately once every 10 million base pairs, but its built-in proofreading mechanism fixes 99% of those errors.
Pellagra, characterized by the '4 Ds' (diarrhea, dermatitis, dementia, death), is caused by a severe deficiency in Niacin (Vitamin B3).
Iron is absorbed in the duodenum primarily in the ferrous (Fe2+) state, which is why taking Vitamin C (an antioxidant) boosts iron absorption.
Bile salts synthesized from cholesterol in the liver act as detergents to emulsify dietary fats in the intestine.
Cytochrome c oxidase, the last enzyme in the respiratory electron transport chain, is inhibited by cyanide, halting ATP production.
Glycogenin is a unique enzyme that acts as both the primer and the catalyst to begin synthesizing a new glycogen molecule in the liver and muscle.
Selenium is inserted into proteins translationally via a unique tRNA (Sec-tRNA) that reads the UGA stop codon as a code for Selenocysteine.
Proteins are tagged for cellular destruction by attaching a small 76-amino-acid protein called ubiquitin.
